source: Invest Like the Best — Investing through a bear market (EP.167)
summary: Software revenue is not truly recurring; software spend will be cut in this recession unlike 2008-09
summary_extended: Baker argues that the widely-held belief in 'truly recurring' software revenue is a myth that will be exposed in the 2020 recession. His structural case rests on a dramatic shift in software's share of IT budgets: in 2009, software represented only 7-10% of IT spend, allowing CIOs to absorb budget cuts elsewhere, but today software accounts for roughly 30% of IT spend (or ~50% including cloud), making it impossible to avoid when budgets are slashed. He cites anecdotal evidence from private equity contacts and a dentist who cut their software bills by 30-50% in under an hour with zero resistance from vendors, suggesting that even SaaS vendors will capitulate rather than lose customers. The investment implication is bearish on high-multiple SaaS and cloud software companies whose valuations are premised on revenue durability and low churn — Robert Smith's famous thesis that 'software is better than first lien debt' is being directly stress-tested. Baker does not name specific short targets but implies broad downside risk across the enterprise and SMB SaaS universe.
detail: In 2009, software was 7-10% of IT spend so CIOs could cut elsewhere. Today software is ~30% of IT spend (50% including cloud). If budgets are cut, software must be affected. Anecdotal evidence: small businesses cutting software bills 30-50% in one hour with zero pushback from vendors. Robert Smith's 'software is better than first lien debt' thesis is being tested.
confidence_evidence: Baker makes a structural argument with specific data points (7-10% vs 30% share), personal anecdotes from PE friends and a dentist, and frames it as a core thesis he has been actively thinking through.
quote_fragment: there is no such thing as truly recurring revenue
